questions.text: I learn from experience instinctively without needing to document or formalize lessons.
I often do not notice when someone changes the subject to avoid discomfort.
I believe my role in any group is to make sure everyone else has what they need.
I tend not to notice social undercurrents unless someone points them out.
I find that thorough preparation gives me an edge in almost every situation.
I see collaboration as a core part of my identity.
I think that structured, analytical engagement with emotionally difficult situations is the approach most likely to produce solutions that stand the test of time.
I notice the way a person's face subtly changes when a sensitive subject comes up.
I can watch an intensely emotional film and feel moved without being shaken.
I feel the strongest moral pull to help the person who is most vulnerable in a group.
I believe that the discipline of objective evaluation during emotionally fraught moments is what separates considered judgment from mere reaction.
I like to wait until a clear consensus emerges before acting.
I am quick to feel a jolt of alarm in ambiguous situations.
I often end up as the de facto leader in group projects.
My emotional responses have a muted quality compared to what I observe in others.
I routinely invest in tools, equipment, and environments that optimize my own performance.
I believe that being too systematic can blind you to novel solutions hiding in plain sight.
I feel that directing my resources toward my own goals is the smartest strategy.
